coredata.dc:title: Aptamer based electrochemical assay for protein kinase activity by coupling hybridization chain reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Li-Ping
Zhao, Ruo-Nan
Wang, Li-Juan
Ma, Rong-Na
Zhang, Wei
Shang, Lei
Wang, Huai-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: The present work reported a simple, lable-free and sensitive electrochemical method for the detection of protein kinase A (PKA) activity. This method was based on the specific recognition of aptamer and the aptamer-induced hybridization chain reaction (HCR) amplification strategy. The aptasensor was constructed by immobilizing capture probe on a gold electrode via an Au–S bond. When adenosine triphosphate (ATP) aptamer was introduced, its one terminus hybridized with capture probe and the other hybridized with the complementary region of an auxiliary probe, which other region triggered HCR between two hairpin DNA (H1 and H2) to form a long DNA concatamer. At last a large number of electroactive methyle blue (MB) molecules were assembled on the dsDNA concatamer, which generated a significantly amplified electrochemical signal. In the presence of ATP, the HCR would not be performed because the aptamer specifically bond to ATP and the electrochemical response would decrease. However, when ATP and PKA coexisted, the electrochemical response would recovery because that ATP had been translated into ADP by PKA. So the activity of PKA could be effectively monitored according to the change of electrochemical signal. Based on the HCR amplification strategy, the aptasensor showed a wide linear range (4 − 4 ×105 U L−1) and a low detection limit (1.5 U L−1) for the detection of PKA. Furthermore, the method was applied to study the inhibitory effect of H-89 on PKA activity. The developed aptasensor was also used to the analysis of drug-induced PKA activity in cell lysates, indicating the potential application of the developed method in the fields of clinical diagnostics and discovery of new targeted drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptamer
PKA activity
Hybridization chain reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Protein kinase activity detection and inhibitor screening
PKA assay in HeLa cell and human normal HcerEpic cell
Performance of the aptasensor to PKA
Selectivity, reproducibility and durability of the electrochemical aptasensor
Detection of PKA activity in cell lysates

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21405070 , 21427808 , 21505063 & 21375055 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) EIS of gold electrode (a); MCH/CP/gold (b); Apt/MCH/CP/gold (c); d: AP/Apt/MCH/CP/gold (d); H1+H2/AP/Apt/MCH/CP/gold (e); H1+H2/AP/Apt/MCH/CP/gold incubated in ATP solution (f). (B) DPV curves of MB/H1+H2/AP/Apt/MCH/CP/gold (a); MB/H1+H2/AP/Apt/MCH/CP/gold incubated in 0.5 mM ATP solution (b); MB/H1 +H2/AP/Apt/MCH/CP/gold incubated in the solution containing 0.5 mM ATP and 4 × 104 U L−1 PKA (c).

The effect of Apt concentration (A), Apt incubation time (B), hybridization time of H1 and H2 (C) on the performance of the aptasensor. (D) The influence of ATP concentration. The concentration of ATP was 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 1500 μM, respectively. The error bars were gained from three parallel test results.

The DPV curves in the presence of different PKA concentrations under the optimized conditions, from a to f: 4, 4 × 10, 4 × 102, 4 × 103, 4 × 104, 4 × 105 U L−1. (Inset was the relationship between the peak current and the logarithm of PKA concentration in the range of 4– 4 ×105 U L−1). The error bars were gained from three parallel test results.

The interference of some enzymes and BSA on the determination of PKA. The PKA concentration was 4 × 105 U L−1 while other enzymes were in five times concentration of PKA (except for 1% BSA). The error bars were gained from three parallel test results.

DPV responses of the aptasensor to different H-89 concentrations (from a to h：0，0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.5, 5, 10 μM. Inset is the inhibition efficiency of H-89. The error bars were gained from three parallel test results
The design principle of the aptasensor. The response signal of the sensor presents an “on-off-on” way for Rout a, b and c, respectively.

head.ce:title: Aptamer based electrochemical assay for protein kinase activity by coupling hybridization chain reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work reported a simple, lable-free and sensitive electrochemical method for the detection of protein kinase A (PKA) activity. This method was based on the specific recognition of aptamer and the aptamer-induced hybridization chain reaction (HCR) amplification strategy. The aptasensor was constructed by immobilizing capture probe on a gold electrode via an Au–S bond. When adenosine triphosphate (ATP) aptamer was introduced, its one terminus hybridized with capture probe and the other hybridized with the complementary region of an auxiliary probe, which other region triggered HCR between two hairpin DNA (H1 and H2) to form a long DNA concatamer. At last a large number of electroactive methyle blue (MB) molecules were assembled on the dsDNA concatamer, which generated a significantly amplified electrochemical signal. In the presence of ATP, the HCR would not be performed because the aptamer specifically bond to ATP and the electrochemical response would decrease. However, when ATP and PKA coexisted, the electrochemical response would recovery because that ATP had been translated into ADP by PKA. So the activity of PKA could be effectively monitored according to the change of electrochemical signal. Based on the HCR amplification strategy, the aptasensor showed a wide linear range (4 − 4 ×105 U L−1) and a low detection limit (1.5 U L−1) for the detection of PKA. Furthermore, the method was applied to study the inhibitory effect of H-89 on PKA activity. The developed aptasensor was also used to the analysis of drug-induced PKA activity in cell lysates, indicating the potential application of the developed method in the fields of clinical diagnostics and discovery of new targeted drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic AMP (cAMP)-dependent protein kinases regulate multiple processes in eukaryotes by phosphorylating diverse cellular substrates, including metabolic and signaling enzymes, ion channels and transcription factors (Natasha et al., 2016). Due to their primary role in cell physiology and pathology, protein kinases have become major drug targets to counteract human diseases, such as heart failure and cancer (Kim et al., 2016). The function of protein kinase A is to transfer the g-phosphate of ATP to Ser/Thr/Tyr residues of substrates, thereby activating or deactivating various signaling pathways (Endicott et al., 2012). So the over expression and aberrant regulation of protein kinases may resulted in severe diseases such as cancer, diabetes, immune diseases, and cardiac diseases (Wang et al., 2012, 2017). Therefore, developing a simple, sensitive and low-cost method for the detection of protein kinase activity is of great importance in clinical diagnosis, targeted therapy and drug discovery (Liu et al., 2017).

At present, the standard method for analyzing protein kinase activity is the autoradiography technique by use of 32P-labeled adenosine triphosphate (Houseman et al., 2002; Hutti et al., 2004). However, the radioactive labels are harmful to the environment and human health. Thus, a few non-radioactive methods have been developed recently for the detection of protein kinase activity, including fluorescence (Yin et al., 2015; Bai et al., 2013), flow cytometry (Ren et al., 2013), quartz crystal-microbalance (Xu et al., 2012), electrochemistry (Zhou et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2012; Marti´c et al., 2012), mass spectrometry (Kim et al., 2007), colorimetric methods (Wang et al., 2006; Oishi et al., 2007), magnetic resonance imaging (Shapiro et al., 2009) as well as electrogenerated chemiluminescence (Xu et al., 2010; Liang et al., 2016). However, most of these methods require laboratory-based expensive and sophisticated instruments such as mass spectrometry and fluorescence. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a simple, low-cost and sensitive method for the assay of protein kinase.

Recently, the electrochemical methods have attracted many attentions on protein kinase assay due to the advantages of simple device, easy operation, inexpensive cost, fast detection, micro-volume sample, and high sensitivity. In a general design of electrochemical kinase biosensors, the kinase-specific peptide substrates are immobilized on an electrode surface. After phosphorylation, the resulting phosphorylated peptides can be recognized by special elements, such as anti-phosphorylated peptide antibodies (Wilner et al., 2008), metal ions (Zr4+, Fe3+, Ag+ and Tb3+) (Miao et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2013, 2014; Yang et al., 2015), TiO2 nanoparticles (Ji et al., 2009) and ATP derivates (Wang et al., 2006; Liu et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2015). These electrochemical methods with peptides as substrates have made great advances for evaluating the activities of protein kinases. However, there are still some deficiencies which need to be overcame. For example, most of these methods need specific peptides as templates, the design strategies are more complex, some biochemical reagents such as anti-phosphorylated peptide antibodies are expensive, and the synthesis of ATP derivatives is difficult and time-consuming.

Aptamers are artificial oligonucleic acids to bind target molecules with high affinity. Compared to other molecular recognition system, aptamers show a lot of advantages such as the simplicity of synthesis, excellent stability, and ease of labeling. Recently the electrochemical aptamer biosensors have attracted significant attention due to the excellent specificity, simple instrument, and fast response (Lu et al., 2008; Zhu et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2014). However, a challenge in electrochemical aptasensor design is to improve the sensitivity for low level target detection. Nowadays, many signal amplification strategies have been developed to improve the detection sensitivity, including hybridization chain reaction (HCR) amplification (Hou et al., 2015; Ge et al., 2014), rolling circle amplification (RCA) (Liu et al., 2013), and isothermal exponential amplification (Liu et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2014). Among them, HCR shows great potential in signal amplification because of its unique features such as simple, cost-effective, isothermal, and sensitive. HCR is an enzyme-free and triggered self-assembly strategy to construct one-dimensional DNA nanostructure. Since HCR was found in 2004, it has been widely used to fabricate a variety of biosensors for the sensitive detection of oligonucleotides (Jia et al., 2016), proteins (Xu et al., 2013), cancer cells (Sharma et al., 2012) and so on (Jia et al., 2018).

Inspired by the aforementioned works, we reported a novel electrochemical aptasensor for evaluating the activity and inhibition of PKA based on aptamer (Apt) recognition and HCR amplification strategy. The presence of Apt can lead to the formation of dsDNA polymers on the sensing surface through in situ HCR. Numerous indicators, methyle blue (MB), thus can be efficiently intercalated into the grooves of the dsDNA polymers to generate the signals. In the presence of ATP, the Apt would bind to ATP with high affinity, which prevented the assembly of HCR on the electrode surface. As a result, a significant decrease in electrochemical signal was observed. However, when PKA was added, the electrochemical signal would recover because that ATP would be translated into ADP by PKA and ADP can not combine with Apt. Based on this “signal-off” and “signal-on” mode, the quantitative assay for ATP and PKA can be readily realized. The traditional assays of PKA activities were typically performed by the phosphorylation of specific peptide substrate then the interaction of the substrates with various signal probes. However, to the best of our knowledge, few studies have been reported for the detection of PKA based on the aptamer recognition (Wang et al., 2017). Importantly, the aptasensor introduced HCR amplification strategy, which enhanced the response sensitivity and decreased the detection limit. Furthermore, the aptasensor was free-labeled and free-enzyme, which enabled the fabrication simple. So this work provided a new strategy for the assay of PKA activity.
All oligonucleotides used in the experiment were synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and their sequences (from 5′ to 3′) are listed in Table S1.

Protein kinase A (PKA, catalytic subunit from bovine heart), adenosine triphosphate (ATP), cytidine triphosphate (CTP), guanosine triphosphate (GTP), uridine triphosphate (UTP), horse radish peroxidase (HRP), glucose oxidase (GOx), exonuclease I (Exo I), exonuclease Ⅱ (Exo Ⅱ), thrombin, bovine serum albumin was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Tris-(2-carboxyethyl)-phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane, 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH), and methyl blue (MB) were purchased from Aladdin Inc. (Shanghai, China). Forskolin, 3-isobutyl-1-methylxanthine (IBMX) and H-89 were purchased from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). One step animal cell active protein extraction kit (BSP022) was purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other reagents such as K3Fe(CN)6，K4Fe(CN)6, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, H2SO4, Na2HPO4，NaH2PO4 were purchased from Shanghai Reagent Corporation (Shanghai, China). All these reagents were of analytical grade and without further purification. All oligonucleotides solutions were prepared with Tris buffers (25 mM Tris–HCl, 100 mM NaCl, 5 mM MgCl2, pH 7.4). Furthermore, the capture probes were reduced in 2 mM TCEP for 1 h to cleave disulfide bonds. Electrochemical measurements was performed in 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4). All solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water (18 mΩ·cm resistivity) from a Millipore system.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed with a CHI 760 C electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China). All electrochemical experiments were performed with a conventional three-electrode system comprising of a gold working electrode, a platinum wire auxiliary electrode, and a Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The DPV measurements were performed in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) by using a potential window from 0 to −0.5 V with scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The potential increments was 4 mV, the amplitude and pulse width were both 0.05 V, and the pulse period was 0.2 s. Before the DPV measurements, the electrolyte solution should be thoroughly purged with high purity nitrogen to avoid the interference of oxygen. EIS measurements were carried out in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl with the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to10 kHz. The amplitude of the applied sine wave potential was 5 mV and the formal potential of the system was set at + 0.22 V.

Briefly, a gold electrode (2 mm in diameter) was polished on a microcloth with 0.05 µm alumina slurry for 5 min, followed by sonicating in ethanol and water for 5 min, respectively. Then the polished electrode was electrochemically cleaned in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution by scanning from −0.3 V to + 1.5 V until a steady-state cyclic voltammogram was obtained. After being thoroughly rinsed with Milli-Q water and dried with nitrogen, the cleaned electrodes should be immediately used for DNA immobilization.

First, 6 μL of 1.0 μM thiolated CP solution was dropped on the cleaned gold electrode surface and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C to form self-assembly monolayers by Au-S bond. Next, the electrode was immersed into 1 mM MCH for 0.5 h to remove the nonspecific DNA adsorption. After rinsed thoroughly and dried with nitrogen, 6 μL of 1.0 μM Apt solution was dropped on the electrode surface for 1.5 h at 37 °C. Then the electrode was covered with 6 μL of 1.0 μM AP solution. After 1.5 h incubation time, the electrode was immersed into the mixture solution containing 1.0 μM H1 and 1.0 μM H2 for 1.5 h to perform HCR. Finally, 6 μL of 1 mM MB was dropped on the electrode surface for 0.5 h at room temperature. The electrode surface was washed with 10 mM tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 100 mM NaCl) and dried with nitrogen after each modification step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein kinase activity detection and inhibitor screening
ce:section.ce:para: For the evaluating of protein kinase activity, the mixture containing 1 mM ATP and different concentrations of PKA were dropped on the above sensing interface for 1 h incubation at 37 °C, followed by washing with the tris-HCl buffer thoroughly. Then the resulting electrode was characterized by DPV in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4).
For a PKA inhibitor assay, the procedures were similar to above, except for different concentrations of inhibitor were added in the mixture of 1 mM ATP and 2 × 105 U L−1 PKA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PKA assay in HeLa cell and human normal HcerEpic cell
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells and human normal HcerEpic cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 (Gibco) containing 10% fetal bovine serum at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 ̶ 95% air incubator, respectively. Before stimulation, the cells were incubated for 4 h in the serum-free medium. Then, the cultured cells were treated with forskolin and IBMX according to a certain proportion (1∶2) to activate the intracellular PKA. After 30 min stimulation, the cells were centrifugated at 3000 rpm for 5 min at 4 ℃ to remove the medium and washed three times with cold PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.4). Subsequently, an animal cell active protein extraction kit (BSP022, Sangon, Shanghai) was used to extract active proteins from the cultured cells: briefly, the cells were lysed in lysis buffer containing protease inhibitor, dithiothreitol (DTT), and phenylmethanesulfonyl fluoride (PMSF). The mixture was sonicated three times for 30 s on ice at intervals of 60 s and then centrifuged at 16000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C. The resulting cell lysate was aliquoted and stored at −20 °C before use. Total protein concentrations in cell lysates were quantified by the modified Lowry protein assay kit with bovine serum albumin as the standard. The total protein concentration of cell lysate was diluted to 6 μg mL−1 for the proposed kinase activity assay.

The design strategy of the proposed aptasensor is exhibited in Scheme 1. Firstly, capture probe was immobilized on gold electrode via Au-S bond. When the ATP aptamer (Apt) was introduced (rout a), a sandwiched hybridization between capture probe (CP) and auxiliary probe (AP) processed. Then the exposed domain of AP would trigger HCR between two complementary hairpin DNA (H1, H2) to yield nicked double-helices DNA polymer. Finally, the electroactive MB moleculers were intercalated into the grooves of the dsDNA polymers to obtain an amplified electrochemical signal. In the presence of ATP (rout b), the Apt would preferentially bind to ATP with high affinity, which could not induce the following HCR on the sensing surface. As a result, a significant decrease in electrochemical signal was observed. However, when PKA was added, ATP could be translated into ADP by PKA and ADP can not combine with ATP Apt. Thus the HCR will continue again and the electrochemical signals will recover. Therefore, we can effectively monitored the activity of protein kinase A according to the variation of electrochemical signal. So the Apt plays an important role and its selectivity to ATP is crucial in the evaluation of PKA activity. We studied the effect of some ATP analogues on the performance of the aptasensor by dropping several solutions containing 100 μM ATP, CTP, UTP and GTP on the surface of MB/H1+H2/AP/Apt/MCH/CP/gold electrode for 1 h incubation at 37 °C, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1 of the Supplementary Material that only ATP induced obvious signal decrease, while CTP, UTP and GTP gave the same signal response as blank. This proves that the ATP-binding aptamer sequence is very specific to ATP.

First, the HCR-based aptasensor fabrication process was confirmed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In a typical EIS, the diameter of semicircle equals to the electron-transfer resistance (R
et ), which reflects the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe ([Fe(CN)6]3-/4-) at the electrode surface. As shown in Fig. 1A, a very small semicircle was observed for bare gold electrode (curve 'a'), indicating a low electron-transfer resistance. The resistance increased with the assembly of CP and MCH on the electrode (curve 'b'), which was ascribed to the phosphate backbone of DNA molecules to repel the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- ions. The resistance continuously increased (curve 'c, d') when the MCH/CP-modified electrode was incubated with Apt and AP step by step. With the introduction of H1 and H2, the nicked double-helices DNA polymer formed on the electrode by alternative hybridization between H1 and H2, which resulted in a large increase of R
et value (curve 'e'). However, the resistance dramatically decreased when the H1+H2/AP/Apt/MCH/CP/gold electrode was incubated in ATP solution (curve 'f'). It could be ascribed to the dissociations of Apt and the long nicked double-helices DNA polymer from electrode due to the specific binding of the aptamer to ATP.

Furthermore, the feasibility of the HCR-based aptasensor was also characterized by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) (Fig. 1B). In the present work, MB was used as an electrochemical indicator to quantitatively bind to the phosphate backbone of DNA via electrostatic interaction. In the absence of ATP, the long nicked dsDNA polymer formed on the electrode by the hybridization between H1 and H2, thus a large number of MB molecules were inserted into the groove of dsDNA and a large peak current was obtained (curve 'a'). Once the ATP was introduced, the binding of Apt to ATP resulted in the dissociations of aptamer and the long nicked double-helices DNA polymer from electrode accompanied by the reduction of MB quantities immobilized on the sensing face, which made the electrochemical signals decrease significantly (curve 'b'). However, when PKA and ATP coexisted, the electrochemical signal recovered (curve 'c') because that ATP can be translated into ADP by PKA and ADP can not combine with ATP aptamer. According to “signal-off” and “signal-on”, we can detect the concentration of ATP and PKA, respectively.

In general, the experimental conditions greatly affect the analytical performance of the as-prepared aptasensor. The concentration and incubation time of Apt played an important role in the performance of the system. As shown in Fig. 2A, the DPV peak currents increased with the increasing of Apt concentration and then tended to level off above 1.0 μM. The electrochemical signals increased when the incubation time of Apt from 15 min to 90 min, indicating a tendency to complete the hybridization with CP. After that, increasing time no longer enhanced the response signal (Fig. 2B). So 1.0 μM Apt and 90 min incubation time were chosen in the experiment.

In this work, the electrochemical signals mainly depended on the carried MB assembled on the long-rang DNA concatamers, which was formed by the HCR reaction between H1 and H2. So, the length of DNA concatamers directly affected the quantities of MB immobilized on the sensing face. Fig. 2C shows the effect of the HCR time on the electrochemical signals of the HCR-based aptasensor. The peak currents first increased with increasing hybridization time, and then tended to level off after 60 min. Thus 60 min was chosen in the experiment.

We further studied the effect of ATP on the electrochemical response of the HCR-based aptasensor. As shown in Fig. 2D, the peak currents gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of ATP. And the peak currents achieved a minimum when the concentration of ATP reached 1 mM, indicating the Apt immobilized on the electrode had bound to ATP completely. Thus, we selected 1 mM ATP as the optimal concentration in the detection of PKA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the aptasensor to PKA
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, when ATP was introduced into the system, it would bind to Apt and the HCR could not process. Thus, the peak currents would decrease dramatically. However, when PKA and ATP co-existed, the electrochemical responses recovered because that ATP had been translated into ADP by PKA and ADP can not combine with ATP Apt. Therefore, the system can be used for the detection of PKA activity. As shown in Fig. 3, the DPV peak currents increased with the increasing of PKA concentration. And the currents were linearly dependent on the logarithm of PKA concentration in the range of 4 ~ 4 × 105 U L−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9992. The linear regression equation was I p /μA= 6.24 log[PKA]+ 17.50. The detection limit was estimated with the equation according to the reference (Ren et al., 2015): [PKA] = 10(Ip−17.50)/6.24, where I p is the mean signal of blank measures+3σ, and σ is the standard deviation of parallel blank measures. The calculated detection limit was 1.5 U L−1. A comparison between our method and other reported methods for PKA determination in detection limit and linear range was summed up in Table S2. It could be seen from Table S2 (in the Supplementary material) that our method had a comparable detection limit and linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and durability of the electrochemical aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a very important parameter to evaluate the performance of a new fabricated biosensor. So we study the selectivity of the aptasensor in the presence of different enzymes such as GOx, HRP, thrombin, Exo I, ExoⅡand BSA. As shown in Fig. 4, an obvious recovery of DPV current was observed in the presence of PKA whereas there is no obvious change in the presence of other enzymes (five-fold of PKA concentration) and 1% BSA. The results demonstrated the present method showed a good selectivity and was suitable for selective detection of PKA.

The reproducibility is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of the biosensor. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the same batch sensors (five sensors fabricated with same process) were 4.28% for 400 U L−1 PKA. The batch-to batch (three batches, every batch contains five sensors) reproducibility was 11.82% for for 400 U L−1 PKA. The results indicated a desirable reproducibility for the as-proposed electrochemical aptasensor. Moreover, the durability of aptasensor was studied by reserving under 4 °C for two weeks. 92.5% of the original peak current to 400 U L−1 PKA can still be left, indicating its good durability.

PKA is involved in the regulation of a variety of cellular processes, which abnormal expression may be implicated in a number of diseases such as cancers. So the identification of PKA inhibitor is valuable for protein kinase-targeted drug discovery and relative diseases therapy. Here, H-89 was selected as the model inhibitor. In the inhibition assay, the PKA activities were evaluated with PKA inhibitor at different concentrations. As presented in Fig. 5, the DPV response of the aptasensor decreased with the increasing concentration of H-89, which indicated the effective inhibition of H-89 to the activity of PKA. The results demonstrated that the proposed strategy has a great potential in quantitatively screening the activity of protein kinase inhibitor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PKA activity in cell lysates
ce:section.ce:para: PKA have a significant impact on cell signaling and signal transduction, so the measurement of PKA in cell lysates is crucial for related research (Yin et al., 2015). Here, HeLa cells and human normal HcerEpic cells were chosen as cell models. Firstly, the cells were stimulated by various concentrations of Forskolin and IBMX, which had often been used as an efficient activator combination to increase the intracellular levels of cAMP and further activate the cAMP-dependent PKA. As shown in Fig. S2, for the unstimulated samples, no obvious change in current signal can be detected relative to the blank control without cell lysates, suggesting the very low PKA activity under normal physiological condition (Ren et al., 2013; Hu et al., 2018). However, the current obviously increased along with the increase of Forskolin/IBMX concentration, indicating the activation of PKA in stimulated cells. In contrast, if the drug-treated cell lysates were incubated with H-89, the response signals would again decrease sharply, indicating that the cell lysate-produced signals indeed originated from the drug-activated PKA but not from other factors. The results proved that the aptasensor can effectively monitor PKA activity from cell lysates induced by drugs.

In summary, a label-free and sensitive assay for evaluating the activity and inhibition of PKA was developed based on the specific recognition of aptamer and the HCR amplification strategy. Compared with the traditional electrochemical methods, this work adopted aptamer as the recognition instead of specific peptide, which avoided the phosphorylation of peptide and made the aptasensor simplification. Furthermore, this aptasensor showed a high sensitivity for the detection of PKA with a low detection limit and a wide linear range by coupling HCR amplification strategy. Importantly, this aptasensor had been well applied to analyze the PKA activity in cell lysates and evaluate the inhibition of PKA inhibitors, showing great potential in drug discovery, kinase-related diseases diagnose and therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21405070, 21427808, 21505063 & 21375055).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2018.06.067.
